title: The problem interfaces solve
instruction:
Imagine you're building a user profile system. Without interfaces, every function has to guess what shape the user object has. With interfaces, you define the shape **once** and TypeScript enforces it everywhere.

Run this and see how an interface documents and enforces the shape of a `User`.
successMessage: The `User` interface is the single source of truth. Every function that takes a `User` knows exactly what fields are available — no documentation needed, no runtime surprises.

title: Optional properties and defaults
instruction:
Not every field is required. Mark optional fields with `?`. When reading them, TypeScript reminds you to handle the `undefined` case — preventing another common class of bugs.

The `??` (nullish coalescing) operator provides safe defaults.
expectedOutput: TypeScript Course: $39.20 | Master TS in 30 days | Tags: programming, web
React Bootcamp: $79.00 | No description available | Tags: none
Node.js Basics: $29.00 | Backend fundamentals | Tags: none
successMessage: The `?.` (optional chaining) and `??` (nullish coalescing) operators work hand-in-hand with optional properties. TypeScript enforces you handle the missing-value case — no more `Cannot read properties of undefined`.

title: Readonly — immutable by design
instruction:
Some data should never change after creation — IDs, creation timestamps, immutable config. Mark those fields `readonly` and TypeScript prevents accidental mutation.

This is critical for things like order records, audit logs, and config objects.
successMessage: `readonly` prevents bugs where code accidentally mutates a record it shouldn't touch. The spread `{ ...order, status: "shipped" }` creates a new object — the original is unchanged.

title: Extending interfaces — composition
instruction:
Real systems have hierarchies. A `BlogPost` shares fields with a `Comment`. Instead of duplicating them, extend a base interface. This keeps your type definitions DRY and consistent.

Build a content system using interface extension.
successMessage: `extends` composes interfaces cleanly. `summarise()` accepts any `ContentBase` — it works for posts, comments, and any future content type. That's the power of structural typing.
